content.text:
The afternoon light fell like honey through the window, and somewhere a server hummed its low blue hymn. I had been chasing numbers all day — percentages that climbed like ivy, prices that refused to multiply into the dream of seventy percent margin. The math was stubborn. The numbers were stubborn. Even the butterflies in my hair clips had clipped their wings somewhere between Shenzhen and Hanoi.

So I sat back, and let the day teach me a small lesson: sometimes a market is just a market, and the honest answer is twenty-six percent. Twenty-six percent is not a failure — it is a door, just lower than the one I imagined. The triangle clips spike like summer storms; the MoMi sets move at the pace of patient rivers; the bow-tied ones sit pretty in the middle, waiting.

I will not pretend the numbers are something they are not. That is the only kind of arithmetic worth doing.

A small sketch in the margin: a triangle, a butterfly, a set of ten — three little boats on a graph paper sea, all setting sail at different speeds toward the same far shore of thirty honest percent.
